disease.name: Hemothorax
disease.note: Blood in the pleural cavity, usually from trauma. Chest pain, dyspnea, decreased breath sounds. Tube thoracostomy needed. The key decisions: when to drain, when to operate. Massive hemothorax (>1500 mL initial output or >200 mL/h ongoing) requires thoracotomy, not just a chest tube. In Kenya, penetrating chest trauma from assaults is common in urban areas.
presentingComplaints: Chest pain after an accident or assault
Fell from height, landed on chest
Dizziness and feeling faint after chest trauma

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Massive/ongoing bleeding -> SEVERE; small stable hemothorax -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Were you stabbed or shot in the chest?
Were you in a road traffic accident?
Did you fall from a height?
Do you have chest pain?
Are you having difficulty breathing?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Have you coughed up blood?
Did you have a central line or chest procedure recently?
Are you on blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin)?
Do you have any bleeding disorder?
Have you lost consciousness at any point?
Do you have pain elsewhere (abdomen, spine)?
What was the mechanism of injury (speed, height, weapon)?
Do you have other injuries?
Can you lie flat or do you need to sit up to breathe?
What was the mechanism of injury (stab, gunshot, boda-boda, car accident, fall)?
Is the shortness of breath getting worse or staying the same?
Do you have chest pain? Is it sharp or dull? Does it hurt to breathe deeply?
Did you lose consciousness at any time?
Where exactly was the injury (which side of the chest)?
How much blood has come out of the chest tube (if placed) since insertion?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
What was the mechanism of injury: blunt (MVC, fall) or penetrating (stab, GSW)?
Was there any initial hemoptysis (coughing up blood)?
Is there difficulty breathing or shortness of breath?
Is there chest pain, and is it pleuritic in nature?
Has there been any episode of hypotension or near-syncope?
How much blood was initially drained if a chest tube was placed?
Has the chest tube output been increasing or decreasing over time?
Does the patient have any coagulopathy or take anticoagulants?
Is there any previous history of chest surgery or pleural disease?
Has the patient had any prior chest tube placements or thoracotomies?

examination_findings.finding: Decreased or absent breath sounds on affected side
Dull percussion on affected side
Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)
Decreased chest wall motion on affected side
Distended neck veins (if tension hemothorax)
Tracheal deviation (away from affected side)
Penetrating wound (entry/exit) on chest
Chest wall contusion or bruising
Rib fractures palpable or visible
Pallor and signs of shock
Subcutaneous emphysema (if associated lung injury)
Decreased SpO2 (<90% on room air)
Cyanosis (late sign)
Decreased breath sounds on the affected side
Dullness to percussion on the affected side
Hypotension and tachycardia (signs of hemorrhagic shock)
Distended neck veins (tension hemothorax with mediastinal shift)
Tracheal deviation away from the affected side (tension physiology)
Chest tube output >1500 mL initially (massive hemothorax)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Assess for decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion on affected side
Monitor chest tube output volume hourly (normal <100 mL/h after placement)
Check for tracheal deviation (suggests massive hemothorax or tension component)
Palpate for chest wall tenderness, crepitus, or flail segment
Assess for jugular venous distension (suggests associated tamponade or tension)
Auscultate for absent or muffled heart sounds (Beck triad: tamponade)
Measure oxygen saturation continuously
Assess for signs of shock: tachycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries, delayed capillary refill
Check for associated injuries: scapula fracture, rib fractures, clavicle fracture
Inspect entry/exit wounds for penetrating trauma and trace trajectory

bedside_scores.name: Chest Trauma Severity Assessment
components: Initial chest drain output >1500 mL (2 pts)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score ≥2: indication for thoracotomy. Score 1: consider VATS or close monitoring.

subtypes.name: Small (<500 mL)
subtypes.management: Large-bore chest tube (28-32 Fr). Monitor output. If resolves, remove tube when output <100-150 mL/day.

subtypes.name: Massive (>1500 mL)
subtypes.management: Emergency thoracotomy. Massive transfusion protocol. Surgical control of bleeding source.

resuscitation: ATLS primary survey with chest assessment
IV crystalloid (limited, permissive hypotension until surgical control)
Blood transfusion if shock (cross-matched or O-negative)
drainage: Chest drain insertion in 5th ICS anterior axillary line
Use 28-32Fr for small, 36-40Fr for large hemothorax
Connect to underwater seal and suction (20 cmH2O)

indications: Ongoing bleeding >200 mL/h for 4 consecutive hours
surgical.approach: Anterolateral thoracotomy (4th or 5th ICS) for emergency
steps: Position: supine with arm abducted, sandbag under shoulder
Anterolateral incision in 4th/5th ICS from sternum to mid-axillary line
Enter pleural cavity, evacuate blood and clot
Identify bleeding source: lung laceration, intercostal vessel, internal mammary, great vessels
Control bleeding: suture ligation, stapled wedge resection, vascular repair
Copious warm saline irrigation
Chest drains ×2 (apical and basal)
Close thoracotomy in layers

pathways.criteria: output <500mL, hemodynamically stable, no ongoing bleeding
pathways.treatment: Chest drain (28-32Fr). Monitor output. Repeat CXR. VATS if retained hemothorax.
Massive transfusion protocol. Large chest drain (36Fr). Emergency thoracotomy (anterolateral). Control bleeding. Autotransfusion.

operative_templates.procedure: Emergency Anterolateral Thoracotomy for Massive Hemothorax
operative_templates.indication: Massive hemothorax (>1500mL initial output, ongoing >200mL/h, or hemodynamic instability)
operative_templates.position: Supine, ipsilateral arm abducted, sandbag under scapula
operative_templates.incision: 4th or 5th intercostal space from sternum to mid-axillary line
steps: Rapid prep and drape of entire chest
Skin incision along the chosen interspace
Divide intercostal muscles with scissors
Enter pleural cavity (expect gush of blood)
Evacuate blood manually and with suction
Identify bleeding source: lung, intercostal, internal mammary, hilum
Lung laceration: repair with 3-0 Vicryl or stapled wedge
Intercostal vessel: suture ligate proximally and distally
Internal mammary: ligate
Irrigate with warm saline
Insert chest drains (1 apical, 1 basal)
Close with pericostal sutures (heavy Vicryl or Ethibond)
Close muscle and skin layers

watchFor: high drain output then abrupt cessation
persistent opacity on CXR despite drain

dischargeCriteria: Chest drain removed (output <100-200 mL/24h, lung expanded)

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Hemothorax means blood has collected in the space between your lung and chest wall, usually from an injury. The blood prevents your lung from expanding fully. A chest tube was placed to drain the blood and let your lung re-expand.
patient_education.warningSigns: Difficulty breathing, chest pain, fever, coughing up blood, drainage from the chest tube site. Seek help immediately.
patient_education.activity: Rest for 2 weeks. Avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks. Gradual return to normal activity. Deep breathing exercises to prevent lung complications.
patient_education.diet: High protein foods to aid healing and blood recovery. Iron-rich foods if anaemic.
patient_education.medication: Pain medications as prescribed. Antibiotics if given. Complete the course.
patient_education.followUp: Return for chest X-ray in 2 weeks and clinic review.

before_discharge: Chest X-ray to confirm lung expansion
Remove chest drain when criteria met

after_discharge.action: Clinical assessment, return to work/activity discussion
Functional assessment, pulmonary function if needed

when_to_return: Redness, swelling, or discharge at drain site

pathophysiologyDetail: Hemothorax is the accumulation of blood in the pleural space, most commonly resulting from trauma (blunt or penetrating) causing injury to the lung parenchyma, intercostal vessels, internal mammary artery, or great vessels. The pathophysiology begins with disruption of vascular structures within the thoracic cavity. Bleeding from low-pressure pulmonary vessels (lung parenchyma) is often self-limiting due to the low-pressure pulmonary circulation and the tamponade effect of the accumulated blood. In contrast, bleeding from systemic arteries (intercostal, internal mammary) can be massive and persistent. The hemothorax causes compression of the ipsilateral lung (restrictive physiology), mediastinal shift toward the contralateral side (compromising venous return and cardiac output), and can lead to hypovolemic shock if blood loss exceeds 1500-2000 mL (massive hemothorax). In the Kenyan context, hemothorax most commonly results from: (1) penetrating trauma — stab wounds (panga/machete, knife) and gunshot wounds are prevalent in urban areas; (2) blunt trauma — boda-boda accidents, motor vehicle collisions, and falls; and (3) iatrogenic causes — central line insertion, thoracentesis, or pleural biopsy. Blunt trauma can cause hemothorax from rib fractures lacerating the lung or intercostal vessels, pulmonary contusion with laceration, or rupture of the great vessels (aortic isthmus injury in rapid deceleration).

The pleural space is a potential cavity normally containing 5-15 mL of serous fluid. Accumulation of blood within this space has both hemodynamic and respiratory consequences. Hemodynamically, blood loss into the pleural space is 'third-space' loss that does not contribute to effective circulating volume. A hemothorax can sequester 1000-3000 mL of blood, enough to cause class III-IV hemorrhagic shock. The mediastinum can shift to the contralateral side with large collections, impeding venous return (obstructive shock physiology similar to tension pneumothorax).

Respiratory consequences include: (1) compression atelectasis of the ipsilateral lung from the mass effect of collected blood, leading to ventilation-perfusion mismatch; (2) restriction of diaphragmatic movement on the affected side; (3) mediastinal shift compressing the contralateral lung in massive collections; and (4) the 'trapped lung' phenomenon if a retained clot organizes and forms a fibrothorax, causing permanent restrictive lung disease.

The natural history of traumatic hemothorax follows three patterns: (1) self-limiting — bleeding stops spontaneously due to low-pressure pulmonary circulation and pleural tamponade (most lung parenchymal injuries); (2) persistent but slow — ongoing ooze requiring tube drainage but not thoracotomy (intercostal vessel injuries); (3) massive/exsanguinating — rapid bleeding from systemic arteries (internal mammary, intercostal) or great vessels requiring immediate thoracotomy.

At the molecular level, blood in the pleural space triggers an inflammatory response with activation of the coagulation and fibrinolytic cascades. The pleural mesothelium releases tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA) and plasminogen activator inhibitor (PAI-1), creating a balance between clot formation and fibrinolysis. If the balance favors clot formation (from high concentrations of PAI-1 released by activated mesothelial cells), a retained hemothorax develops with organization into a fibrothorax over 4-6 weeks. This is why early drainage (within 72 hours) of retained hemothorax is critical.

The 'retained hemothorax' is defined as residual blood collection >300-500 mL after chest tube placement, seen on CT or CXR. The incidence is 20-30% after tube thoracostomy for trauma. Retained hemothorax serves as a nidus for infection (empyema rate 25-30% if not drained) and causes pleural thickening. Early VATS (within 72 hours) for retained hemothorax reduces empyema rates and hospital length of stay.

In Kenya, hemothorax most commonly results from: (1) penetrating chest trauma — panga/machete wounds, knife stabs, and gunshot wounds are prevalent in urban areas and during inter-community conflicts; (2) blunt trauma from boda-boda accidents, MVAs, and falls; (3) iatrogenic causes — central line insertions, thoracentesis, and pleural biopsies performed without ultrasound guidance; and (4) spontaneous rupture of intercostal or internal mammary vessels in patients on anticoagulation. Open pneumothorax ('sucking chest wound') from panga attacks is a unique Kenyan presentation requiring immediate occlusive dressing.

The pathophysiology of massive hemothorax leading to cardiac arrest is a combination of: (1) hypovolemic arrest from loss of >50% circulating blood volume, (2) obstructive arrest from mediastinal shift compressing the heart and great vessels, and (3) tension physiology if the pleural defect acts as a one-way valve (tension hemothorax). Emergency department thoracotomy (EDT) for cardiac arrest from massive hemothorax has survival rates of 5-15% for penetrating trauma and <5% for blunt trauma.

The role of autotransfusion in traumatic hemothorax is underappreciated. Blood drained from the pleural space can be collected using a sterile collection system with citrate anticoagulant (CPD or 10 mL of 4% sodium citrate per 100 mL of blood). Autotransfused blood has lower 2,3-DPG and platelet counts but provides functional RBCs with normal oxygen-carrying capacity. In resource-limited settings, autotransfusion can reduce allogeneic blood requirements by 30-50%.

Coagulopathy in hemothorax is multifactorial: (1) consumption of coagulation factors, (2) dilution from crystalloid and PRBC resuscitation, (3) hypothermia impairing enzyme function, (4) acidosis reducing platelet function and clotting factor activity (the 'lethal triad'), and (5) trauma-induced coagulopathy (TIC) from tissue hypoperfusion activating protein C and inhibiting PAI-1, leading to hyperfibrinolysis. This is why damage control resuscitation with 1:1:1 ratios and TXA is critical in massive hemothorax.

landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 580 patients with traumatic hemothorax across 10 US trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Tube thoracostomy alone vs early VATS for retained hemothorax (>300 mL residual)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early VATS reduced empyema (OR 0.28) and length of stay (7 vs 14 days) compared to additional tube thoracostomy
landmarkTrials.impact: Retained hemothorax >300 mL after initial chest tube should be managed with early VATS to prevent empyema
landmarkTrials.reference: DuBose JJ, et al. Retained hemothorax after tube thoracostomy: outcomes of early VATS vs additional drainage. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2018;84(5):753-760.

landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of tube thoracostomy practices
landmarkTrials.intervention: Large-bore (28-32 Fr) vs small-bore (14-20 Fr) chest tubes for traumatic hemothorax
landmarkTrials.outcome: Large-bore tubes are recommended for hemothorax; small-bore tubes recommended for simple pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.impact: Size matters: 28-32 Fr for blood, 14-20 Fr for air
landmarkTrials.reference: ATLS Subcommittee. Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) Guidelines. 10th ed. 2018.

landmarkTrials.trial: PROPPR Trial (Plasma:Platelet:RBC Ratios in Massive Transfusion)
landmarkTrials.population: 680 severely injured patients requiring massive transfusion
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in 24h mortality; fewer deaths from exsanguination with 1:1:1 group
landmarkTrials.impact: 1:1:1 ratio is standard for massive transfusion in hemorrhagic shock from hemothorax
landmarkTrials.reference: Holcomb JB, et al. The PROPPR randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;313(5):471-482.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRYOSTAT-2 Trial (Cryoprecipitate in Major Trauma Hemorrhage)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,604 trauma patients with major hemorrhage requiring massive transfusion across 25 UK trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early cryoprecipitate (2 pools, 10 units) within 90 minutes of admission vs standard care
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant reduction in 28-day mortality (25.3% vs 26.1%); subgroup analysis showed benefit in patients with penetrating trauma and those receiving >6 units PRBC
landmarkTrials.impact: Routine early cryoprecipitate in all massive transfusion is not supported; consider selectively for fibrinogen <1.5 g/L or in patients needing ongoing massive transfusion
landmarkTrials.reference: Maegele M, et al. CRYOSTAT-2: a multicentre RCT of early cryoprecipitate in major trauma. Lancet. 2023;401(10372):365-374.

landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter observational study of 2,200 trauma patients with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standardized massive transfusion protocol with balanced resuscitation vs uncontrolled resuscitation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Balanced resuscitation (1:1:1 ratio) reduced 24h mortality (15% vs 25%) and reduced total blood product requirements
landmarkTrials.impact: Balanced resuscitation should be initiated early in patients with massive hemothorax and hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Cannon JW, et al. RESCUE: resuscitation in traumatic hemorrhage. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2020;89(3):469-478.

landmarkTrials.trial: VATS vs Thoracotomy for Retained Hemothorax: AAST Multi-institutional Study
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective cohort of 720 patients with retained hemothorax after failed tube thoracostomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: VATS vs repeat thoracostomy vs thoracotomy for retained hemothorax
landmarkTrials.outcome: VATS success rate 82% for primary management; thoracotomy reserved for VATS failures; VATS reduced LOS (7 vs 14 days) and empyema (10% vs 30%)
landmarkTrials.impact: VATS is the procedure of choice for retained hemothorax; thoracotomy for VATS failures or organized fibrothorax
landmarkTrials.reference: DuBose JJ, et al. Management of retained hemothorax: AAST multicenter study. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2020;88(5):650-658.

landmarkTrials.trial: EAST Practice Management Guidelines for Tube Thoracostomy in Trauma
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 42 studies on tube thoracostomy practices in trauma
landmarkTrials.intervention: Recommendations for chest tube size, insertion technique, antibiotic prophylaxis, and drainage management
landmarkTrials.outcome: Antibiotic prophylaxis with cefazolin reduces empyema (OR 0.4); large-bore tubes (28-32 Fr) recommended for hemothorax; small-bore (14-20 Fr) for simple pneumothorax
landmarkTrials.impact: EAST guidelines: prophylactic antibiotics with chest tube insertion reduce infectious complications; tube size should match the clinical indication
landmarkTrials.reference: Cothren CC, et al. EAST guidelines for tube thoracostomy in trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2020;89(1):150-158.

landmarkTrials.trial: Brenner et al. REBOA in Trauma (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter registry of 285 trauma patients undergoing REBOA for hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.intervention: REBOA zone 1 (supraceliac) vs zone 3 (infrarenal) for hemorrhage control
landmarkTrials.outcome: Zone 3 REBOA for pelvic/thoracic hemorrhage: survival 40%; zone 1 REBOA for abdominal hemorrhage: survival 25%; complication: acute kidney injury 20%, limb ischemia 5%
landmarkTrials.impact: REBOA is an adjunct for non-compressible torso hemorrhage; zone 3 placement preferred for pelvic and thoracic sources to minimize ischemic complications
landmarkTrials.reference: Brenner M, et al. REBOA in trauma: AAST AORTA registry. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2018;84(5):758-765.

evidenceTable.intervention: Early VATS vs additional chest tube
VATS vs repeat chest tube vs thoracotomy
evidenceTable.outcome: VATS success 82%; reduced LOS vs thoracotomy
Empyema reduction OR 0.4 with prophylactic antibiotics
No 24h mortality difference; faster hemostasis with 1:1:1

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: CXR/FAST -> TUBE THORACOSTOMY -> MONITOR OUTPUT -> DECIDE (OR vs VATS vs OBSERVE). CXR: blunted costophrenic angle (200-500 mL), opacification (500-1500 mL), complete opacification with mediastinal shift (>1500 mL massive hemothorax). FAST: anechoic collection in pleural space. Tube thoracostomy: 28-32 Fr chest tube positioned posteriorly and apically. Initial output >1500 mL (massive) = OR for thoracotomy. Output >200 mL/hr for 4 hours = OR. Small residual (<300 mL after drainage) = observe. Retained >300 mL = VATS within 72 hours.

managementPearls.pearl: Massive hemothorax (initial output >1500 mL) requires emergency thoracotomy. Do not waste time with further imaging — the patient needs operative control of the bleeding source (intercostal vessel, internal mammary, great vessel, or hilar injury). Call the OR and prepare for blood transfusion simultaneously.

The chest tube should be directed posteriorly (for blood) and apically (for air). A common error is positioning the tube too far inferiorly or anteriorly, resulting in incomplete drainage. The tube should be inserted in the 'safe triangle' (anterior to the latissimus dorsi, posterior to the pectoralis major, above the diaphragm) and advanced 10-15 cm.

Autotransfusion should be considered in every massive hemothorax. Collect chest tube output in an autotransfusion system (e.g., Pleur-evac with autotransfusion bag) using citrate anticoagulant. Contraindications: contaminated hemothorax from open chest wounds or associated bowel injury (risk of bacterial contamination), or known malignancy. In resource-limited settings, use a sterile IV giving set and collection bag with 10 mL of 4% sodium citrate per 100 mL of blood.

Chest tube insertion in the 'triangle of safety' (bordered by the anterior border of latissimus dorsi, the lateral border of pectoralis major, and the 5th intercostal space) minimizes the risk of injury to the diaphragm, liver, and spleen. The tube should be directed posteriorly (for blood) and apically (for air). Confirm position with CXR after insertion: the tube should be visible within the pleural cavity, not in the fissure, not kinked, and all side holes should be intrathoracic.

Prophylactic antibiotics for chest tube insertion: a single dose of cefazolin 2g IV (or clindamycin 600mg if penicillin-allergic) within 1 hour before tube insertion reduces the risk of empyema by 60% (NNT=15). Continue antibiotics for the duration of tube presence is NOT recommended — single dose prior to insertion is sufficient.

The decision for thoracotomy in hemothorax: indicators include initial output >1500 mL (massive hemothorax), ongoing output >200 mL/h for 4 consecutive hours, >1500 mL in 24h, or hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation. However, in the Kenyan context where blood products may be limited, the threshold for thoracotomy should be lower — consider strongly with initial output >1000 mL if ongoing transfusion is not possible.

In patients with retained hemothorax who are not candidates for VATS (lack of equipment or expertise), ultrasound-guided pigtail catheter drainage and intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy (alteplase 10 mg + DNase 5 mg) can resolve up to 70% of retained collections. This is a useful alternative in resource-limited settings where VATS capability is unavailable.

Empyema after hemothorax: if the patient develops fever, leukocytosis, and persistent pleural opacity at day 5-7 post-injury, suspect empyema. Diagnostic thoracentesis showing purulent fluid with pH <7.2, glucose <40 mg/dL, and positive Gram stain confirms. Treatment: chest tube drainage (if not already present), IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole), and VATS decorication if tube drainage fails.

operativeNuances.nuance: Posterolateral thoracotomy (4th or 5th intercostal space) is the standard approach for traumatic hemothorax requiring surgical control. For right-sided injuries, extend the incision from the anterior axillary line to the paravertebral area. For left-sided injuries, the approach may need to be more anterolateral if great vessel injury is suspected. Upon entering the chest, evacuate all clot and blood, identify the source of bleeding (run the hilum, intercostal spaces, internal mammary artery, lung surface), clamp or suture the bleeding vessel, and repair any lung laceration with 3-0 prolene sutures (simple or mattress). Leave two chest drains (apical and basal) to ensure complete drainage.

The anterolateral thoracotomy is the incision of choice for emergency thoracotomy in trauma. Position the patient supine with the ipsilateral arm abducted. Incise from the sternum to the mid-axillary line in the 4th intercostal space (submammary crease in females). For left-sided exposure (cardiac injury, left hilar injury), the incision may be extended across the sternum (clamshell thoracotomy) for bilateral chest access. Divide the intercostal muscles with heavy scissors, enter the pleura, and use a rib spreader to open the interspace. The lung is retracted posteriorly to expose the hilum and mediastinum.

VATS technique for retained hemothorax: place the patient in lateral decubitus position with single-lung ventilation (double-lumen endotracheal tube or bronchial blocker). Use 3 ports: a 10 mm camera port in the 7th or 8th ICS mid-axillary line, and two working ports in the 4th-5th ICS anterior and posterior axillary lines. Evacuate all clot using suction and grasping forceps. If the clot is organized (more than 7-10 days old), decorication using peanut dissectors and blunt dissection in the extrapleural plane may be necessary. Place an intercostal drain through the inferior port site. If <300 mL of residual blood remains and the lung is fully re-expanded, the VATS is successful.

For intercostal vessel bleeding identified at thoracotomy: place a suture ligature (2-0 silk on a curved needle) encircling the intercostal bundle — pass the needle around the rib below the vessel, then around the rib above the vessel, and tie securely. The intercostal artery runs in the subcostal groove of the rib, which provides some protection from laceration. However, the collateral circulation runs through the intercostal muscle, so both the main artery and the collateral must be ligated. For internal mammary artery bleeding: ligate the vessel proximally and distally at the level of the injury.

In the open chest, a lung laceration can be repaired with a running 3-0 Prolene or Vicryl suture in a simple continuous or horizontal mattress pattern. For deep lacerations, perform a stapled wedge resection using an Endo GIA or TA stapler (load thickness: 4.8 mm for healthy lung, 3.5 mm for emphysematous lung). Be careful to not incorporate the bronchus or pulmonary vessels in the staple line. Test the repair by filling the chest with warm saline and ventilating to 30 cmH2O — look for air leaks and oversew any bubbles with 4-0 Prolene.

In the Kenyan context where delayed presentation of massive hemothorax is common, the patient may have a large, organized hemothorax with a trapped lung that is >7-14 days old. Open decorication via posterolateral thoracotomy is the procedure of choice. The approach: incise the pleural reflection at the chest wall (extrapleural plane), develop a plane between the fibrotic rind and the visceral pleura using blunt dissection (peanut or finger), and decorticate the lung completely. Decortication must allow the lung to fully re-expand. Bleeding from the raw chest wall surface is controlled with electrocautery. Place two large chest drains (36 Fr) — one apical, one basal. Post-operative care: incentive spirometry, deep breathing, chest physiotherapy, and adequate pain control to prevent re-accumulation.

Clamshell thoracotomy (bilateral anterior thoracotomy with transverse sternotomy) is the incision of choice for patients in extremis from bilateral hemothorax or when the source of bleeding is unknown. Position: supine with arms abducted. Incise from the right to left mid-axillary line in the 4th or 5th intercostal space. Divide the sternum transversely using a Lebsche knife or Gigli saw. Open the chest like a 'clamshell' by retracting both sides. This provides excellent exposure of both pleural cavities, the heart, great vessels, and bilateral hila. Close with sternal wires for the sternum and pericostal sutures for the rib spaces.

prognosis.mortality: Massive hemothorax: 15-30%; isolated hemothorax: 3-5%; retained hemothorax with empyema: 5-10%
prognosis.kenyanMortality: Estimated 20-40% for massive hemothorax due to transport delays and limited OR/thoracotomy capacity
prognosis.complicationRate: Retained hemothorax: 20-30%; empyema: 5-10%; need for thoracotomy/decorication: 5-15%
prognosis.recoveryTimeline: Chest tube removed 2-5 days; return to normal activities 4-8 weeks; full lung expansion confirmed on follow-up CXR at 6 weeks
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Initial chest tube output (>1500 mL = need for thoracotomy), ongoing bleeding rate (>200 mL/h for 4h = surgical indication), presence of retained clot, and associated injury severity
